ground_truth.title: Book Reviews
ground_truth.main_content: Book Reviews listing page containing multiple reviews of children's and young adult literature. Featured reviews include: Review of Dear Acorn (Love, Oak): Letter Poems to Friends by Joyce Sidman; illus. by Melissa Sweet - In eight pairs of poems, Sidman creates dialogues between two subjects, some in nature (cloud and droplets), some human-made elements (button and coat). Board Book Roundup: That's About the Size of It by Rachel G. Payne - An average of six-by-six inches is what I see most often. It's a good size for perusing, carrying, and even stacking by board books' very young audience. Review of Trapped in the Tar Pit: How Paleontologists Unearthed a City's Prehistoric Past by Jessica Stremer; illus. by Alexander Vidal - The fascinating story of the La Brea Tar Pits gets a wonderful introduction in Stremer's (Plight of the Pelican, rev. 7/20). Review of The Winter of the Dollhouse by Laura Amy Schlitz - Tiph is a child who is pulled in many directions. She loves her younger half siblings but is fed up with the chaos of a house with babies. Review of Moon Song by Michaela Goade; illus. by the author - As a family goes to bed on a cold night, a young girl begins a story to ease her cousin to sleep. The tale unfolds as a walk through the world around them. Fanfare 2025 Annotations by Horn Book - There's nothing like a good library haul! Here's mine, following the January/February 2026 Horn Book Magazine issue-to-press date. It's the issue that begins our hundred-and-second year and contains all the 2025 Boston Globe–Horn Book Awards speeches along with Fanfare. Review of Imogen: The Life and Work of Imogen Cunningham by Elizabeth Partridge; illus. by Yuko Shimizu - Imogen Cunningham (1883–1976) was born into a large, loving family in the Pacific Northwest at the tail end of the nineteenth century. Fanfare 2025 extras - Read more by and about some of our Fanfare 2025 authors and illustrators below. Review of The Last Resort by Erin Entrada Kelly; illus. by Naomi Franquiz - In this supernatural murder mystery, the versatile Kelly (2025 Newbery medalist for The First State of Being, rev. 5/24) skillfully balances otherworldly horrors with more realistic concerns.
with: In eight pairs of poems, Sidman creates dialogues between two subjects, some in nature (cloud and droplets), some human-made elements (button and coat).
An average of six-by-six inches is what I see most often. It's a good size for perusing, carrying, and even stacking by board books' very young audience.
The fascinating story of the La Brea Tar Pits gets a wonderful introduction in Stremer's (Plight of the Pelican, rev. 7/20).
Tiph is a child who is pulled in many directions. She loves her younger half siblings but is fed up with the chaos of a house with babies.
As a family goes to bed on a cold night, a young girl begins a story to ease her cousin to sleep. The tale unfolds as a walk through the world around them.
